era_context: The year of the English Restoration, marking the end of the Interregnum (1649–1660) and the return of the monarchy under Charles II. The Qing Dynasty consolidating Manchu rule in China following the conquest of the Ming. The Ottoman Empire in the Köprülü Vizierate period of administrative reform and military recovery. European colonial expansion in the Americas, Africa, and Asia accelerating. The Scientific Revolution in Europe gaining institutional support.

events.title: English Restoration: Charles II returns to throne
events.description: King Charles II returned to England and was restored to the throne on 29 May 1660, ending the Interregnum (1649–1660) and the Commonwealth under Oliver Cromwell and Richard Cromwell. Charles had been in exile since his father's execution in 1649. His restoration was celebrated with great ceremony and marked a turning point in English history. The Restoration brought the return of the monarchy, the established church, and the House of Lords. However, the Restoration settlement was a compromise: Parliament retained significant power, and the religious settlement was contested. The year 1660 is a watershed moment in English and British history, marking the end of the revolutionary period and the beginning of a new era of constitutional development.

sources.name: Contemporary English court records and parliamentary documents
Samuel Pepys, Diary, entries for May 1660

events.certainty_note: Charles II's restoration date (29 May 1660) is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Royal Society of London founded; institutionalization of science
events.description: The Royal Society of London was founded in 1660, emerging from informal meetings of natural philosophers that had been held during the Interregnum. The Society was formally established to promote the advancement of natural knowledge through experimentation and observation. Early members included Robert Boyle, John Evelyn, Christopher Wren, and others. The Society received a royal charter in 1662, but its founding in 1660 marks the beginning of the institutionalization of science in England. The Royal Society became a centre for the Scientific Revolution and influenced the development of science throughout Europe. The Society's motto, 'Nullius in verba' (Take nobody's word for it), emphasized empirical observation over authority.

sources.name: Royal Society records and meeting minutes

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Royal Society in 1660 is well-documented, though the exact date of the first meeting is debated (some sources cite 1659).

events.title: Treaty of Madrid signed; Spain and Portugal negotiate peace
events.description: Negotiations between Spain and Portugal regarding the Portuguese Restoration War (1640–1668) were ongoing in 1660, though a formal treaty was not signed until 1668. However, 1660 marks a significant moment in Portuguese-Spanish relations, as both powers were weakened by the Thirty Years' War and internal conflicts. The Portuguese Restoration War would continue until 1668, but the diplomatic trend in 1660 was toward eventual recognition of Portuguese independence. The year 1660 represents a turning point in Iberian geopolitics, as Spain's hegemony was declining and Portugal was consolidating its independence.
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Portuguese-Spanish negotiations is well-documented, though 1660 is a year within a longer conflict rather than a decisive moment.

events.title: Shunzhi Emperor dies; Kangxi Emperor to ascend in 1661
events.description: The Shunzhi Emperor (r. 1643–1661), the second Qing emperor, died on 5 February 1661 (some sources date his death to late 1660, but the official date is 5 February 1661). The Shunzhi Emperor had reigned for only 18 years and was succeeded by his young son, the Kangxi Emperor. The Shunzhi Emperor's reign was marked by the consolidation of Qing rule over China following the Manchu conquest. His death marked the end of an era and the beginning of the Kangxi Emperor's long and transformative reign. The transition was managed by the Empress Dowager Xiaozhuang and the Four Regents.

sources.name: Kangxi shilu (Veritable Records of the Kangxi Emperor)

events.certainty_note: The Shunzhi Emperor's death date is well-documented in Qing official records, though some sources date it to late 1660 and others to early 1661.

events.title: Act of Indemnity and Oblivion passed; amnesty for Interregnum participants
events.description: The English Parliament passed the Act of Indemnity and Oblivion in 1660, providing amnesty for most participants in the English Civil War and Interregnum (1642–1660), with the exception of those directly involved in the execution of Charles I. The act was crucial in preventing a cycle of revenge and recrimination that could have destabilized the newly restored monarchy. The act represented a pragmatic compromise between the desire for justice and the need for political stability. However, the act was not universal: those who had signed the death warrant of Charles I were excluded from amnesty, and some were executed. The act established the legal framework for the Restoration settlement.
key_figures: Edward Hyde, Earl of Clarendon (chief minister)

sources.name: Act of Indemnity and Oblivion, 1660 (primary legislation)

events.certainty_note: The Act of Indemnity and Oblivion is well-documented in parliamentary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The English Restoration is sometimes portrayed as a clean break from the Interregnum, but recent scholarship emphasizes continuity in many areas of governance and policy. The Royal Society is sometimes presented as a purely scientific institution, but it was also a political and social institution that reflected Restoration ideology. The Act of Indemnity and Oblivion is sometimes portrayed as purely merciful, but it also served to consolidate royalist power and excluded many from compensation. The Shunzhi Emperor's death date is sometimes given as late 1660 rather than early 1661, reflecting uncertainty in the conversion between the Chinese lunar calendar and the Gregorian calendar.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1660 is rich in European sources, particularly English, reflecting the abundance of parliamentary records, court documents, and contemporary diaries. Chinese sources are well-preserved but filtered through Qing official narratives. The year is well-attested in European political and intellectual history but less so in other regions. The significance of events is weighted toward European political history; the Shunzhi Emperor's death is equally significant for East Asian history but may be underrepresented in English-language historiography. The Royal Society's founding is well-documented in English sources but the broader context of scientific development in other regions (China, the Islamic world) is less thoroughly covered. The Restoration is extensively documented in English sources but the experience of those excluded from amnesty or those who lost property is less thoroughly preserved.

graph_edges.note: Charles II's restoration in 1660 began his 25-year reign
Royal Society's founding in 1660 established institutional framework for Newton's later work
Diplomatic negotiations in 1660 led to Treaty of Madrid in 1668
Shunzhi Emperor's death in 1660/1661 led to Kangxi Emperor's accession
Act of Indemnity and Oblivion in 1660 was followed by Acts of Settlement and Indemnity in 1661
